Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit chaotic but I made it work. I'm 26 and working while studying, so I had to squeeze in study time whenever I could find it. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd often set up study sessions where I could practice things physically - writing out notes, creating diagrams, walking around while reviewing concepts. I tried to dedicate about 26 hours a week, usually in chunks during evenings and weekends when I wasn't exhausted from work.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening at work. Some weeks I'd be consistent, studying a few hours each evening, but then a big deadline or a tough week at work would throw everything off. I'd end up cramming more hours right before assignments were due. It wasn't ideal, but with limited resources and time, I had to be flexible and adapt to whatever life threw at me.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew something was due soon or when I needed to check if there were new announcements. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if I'd missed anything important, especially since my attendance wasn't always perfect. The anxiety of potentially missing something crucial would push me to check in regularly, even if I didn't always have time to engage deeply with the content right then.
Q4: After a few weeks, logging in became more automatic for me, especially in the mornings before work or right after dinner. I'd grab my coffee, open the LMS, and at least scan through what was happening. It became part of my routine because I realized falling behind would make my anxiety worse. Having that consistent check-in helped me feel more in control even when everything else felt overwhelming.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly helpful for me - I watched over three hours worth because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace. As a kinesthetic learner, I'd often watch videos while taking physical notes or even standing up. The peer interactions were also valuable; connecting with other students helped me feel less isolated and gave me different perspectives on the material that I wouldn't have gotten alone.
Q6: I tended to skip or delay the discussion posts because honestly, with my anxiety levels, putting my thoughts out there publicly felt intimidating. I only posted twice the whole semester. I also didn't really explore optional readings or supplementary materials much because I had to prioritize what was absolutely necessary. When you're working with limited time and resources, you learn to focus on what directly impacts your grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to passing the course or understanding something I'd actually use. If I could physically engage with it somehow - like practicing problems, creating something tangible, or applying concepts to real situations - that made it feel valuable. Activities that felt like busywork or that I couldn't connect to the bigger picture were harder to justify when I was already stretched thin.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different - they were like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required activities had this weight to them, this pressure that I had to complete them no matter what. Optional ones I'd look at and think 'that would be nice' but then reality would hit and I'd have to choose between an optional reading and getting enough sleep. The required ones triggered my anxiety more, but at least that anxiety pushed me to complete them.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings to my phone so I could study during my commute or lunch breaks at work. I filled notebooks with handwritten notes because writing things out physically helps me remember better. I also used free resources from the library and YouTube videos to supplement what I was learning. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual study time.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes for hours, practicing problems on paper, or even explaining concepts out loud to myself while doing chores. None of that showed up anywhere. I'd also study with a couple of classmates in person sometimes, working through difficult concepts together, but the LMS would just show I hadn't logged in that day. My real learning often happened away from the screen.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading or download a video once, then study it offline for days. With my limited internet access and the need to study during commutes or breaks, downloading everything I could in one session made sense. I'd watch a lecture video once online, download it, and then rewatch it three or four more times offline while taking notes. The LMS only saw that first access.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially when my anxiety spiked or when work got really demanding. Around midterms, I remember feeling completely overwhelmed - the workload piled up and I couldn't keep up with everything. My attendance dropped during those times because I had to make impossible choices between work, studying, and just trying to manage my mental health. The CBT sessions helped, but those weeks were rough.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. When I was fatigued from work, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion about course material made me avoid certain sections because I didn't know where to start. My competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between engaging deeply with the LMS or just skimming through to check boxes. It wasn't ideal, but survival mode is real.
Q14: Yes, there were definitely times I'd have the LMS open but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be watching a video but thinking about work problems or feeling too anxious to concentrate. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to mark them as viewed, planning to come back later when I could actually focus. My body was present but my brain was dealing with too many other things to truly engage.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I'd like to admit, even knowing they were important. Sometimes the anxiety about doing something imperfectly paralyzed me - I'd rather avoid it than face the possibility of failing. Other times I was just too exhausted to tackle something challenging, so I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I had more energy. The procrastination usually made my anxiety worse, but in the moment it felt like the only option.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd have periods of low activity and then intense bursts right before something was due. The deadline pressure was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and force me to act. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, checking and double-checking submission requirements. It wasn't healthy, but the structure of deadlines gave me external motivation when my internal motivation was depleted.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback would boost my mood and make me more likely to engage with the next assignment. But critical feedback, even when constructive, would sometimes trigger my anxiety and make me want to avoid the course for a while. I'd need time to process it before I could use it productively. The timing mattered too - feedback that came quickly helped me adjust, but delayed feedback felt disconnected from my effort.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear deadlines gave me something concrete to work toward, which I needed. But the pacing sometimes felt relentless when combined with my work schedule. I appreciated when modules built on each other logically because that helped me see the bigger picture. The structure pushed me to stay somewhat consistent even when I wanted to give up.
Q19: The video content being available to download made engagement much easier for me given my situation. Having peer interaction opportunities also helped because I could connect with others going through the same thing. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or when there wasn't flexibility in how I could demonstrate understanding. As a kinesthetic learner, purely reading-based activities were harder to engage with meaningfully.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce work that meets the standards. Maybe they're anxious about submitting something imperfect, or they're confused about requirements. It could also be someone dealing with personal issues that drain their capacity to complete tasks even though they're trying to stay connected to the course. High activity might reflect effort and intention even when output is low.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. A lot of my learning happened offline - in my notebooks, during conversations with peers outside the platform, while I was commuting. Someone might appear to have low LMS activity but be deeply engaged with downloaded materials or studying from notes. The LMS doesn't capture the hours I spent with physical materials or the understanding I built through hands-on practice.
Q22: I think course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing around deadlines, and which resources people click on. But they miss the quality of engagement - whether someone was actually focused or just had the page open. They miss all the offline studying, the emotional state of the student, and whether the learning actually stuck. They show behavior but not understanding or effort that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and putting in effort to learn, even when it's hard. For me, it's about showing up mentally, not just physically clicking through things. It's wrestling with difficult concepts, asking questions, connecting ideas to real life. Engagement is active participation in my own learning, whether that shows up in a system or not. It's choosing to keep going even when anxiety tells me to quit.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite my anxiety and limited resources. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying on buses, the notebooks I filled, or the peer study sessions at the library. It wouldn't capture the emotional journey - the moments I almost gave up, the CBT sessions that helped me cope, or the pride I felt when I finally understood something difficult. My resilience and determination are invisible in the data.
